Existing Website Admin / ERP Integration Architecture Blueprint
Tailored to current React admin + Express backend

Integrated enterprise retail ERP inside the existing admin dashboard

The right path is not to launch a second ERP product. Your current project already has the foundations of an in-app ERP shell, so the correct move is to deepen that shell into a modular retail platform that stays under the same website, the same login, the same admin route tree, and the same user session.

Entry point`/admin/*` stays the only admin surface Frontend baseVite + React SPA with client-side routing Backend baseExpress + PostgreSQL foundation already present Target evolutionNestJS modules behind the same domain Auth ruleZero second login and zero iframe usage Scope modelBusiness, store, and warehouse aware
Second website
0
Required
Everything remains under the current application boundary
Second login
0
Required
The current auth context and protected admin shell stay in place
Delivery waves
4
Recommended
Progressive rollout without breaking the live commerce site
Architecture stance
One app
Native ERP
Shared routes, shared layout, shared auth, shared permissions

Current readiness by capability area

Proposed rollout coverage by delivery wave

Executive decision

Do not split the ERP into a separate product. Your current frontend already routes all admin work through `ProtectedRoute` on `/admin/*`, and the current admin dashboard already mounts role-aware ERP navigation with module-level access control. That means the core application-shell decision has already been made correctly inside the codebase.

The real gap is domain depth, not shell integration. The project already contains tenant middleware, ERP routes, ERP tables, role permissions, and an integrated sidebar. What it does not yet have is the full transactional engine for POS, stock ledger integrity, approvals, accounting, immutable auditing, printer orchestration, and enterprise-grade settings management.

The ERP should be delivered as a deepening of the existing admin dashboard, not as a new admin system wearing the same logo.

Current fit-gap

Observed in codebase
Area What already exists What is still missing Architectural implication
Admin shell `App.jsx` already protects `/admin/*`; `AdminDashboard.jsx` already renders a left ERP sidebar and internal route switching. Most ERP modules still render a generic workspace instead of production workflows. The shell should be preserved and expanded, not replaced.
Authentication `AuthContext.jsx` and `ProtectedRoute.jsx` already enforce shared login, role checks, and permission checks. No refresh-token lifecycle, device trust, or step-up authentication for sensitive actions yet. Keep current login entry point and upgrade the session model behind it.
RBAC Roles and permissions already exist in `src_users`, `src_permissions`, and `src_role_permissions`. Permission enforcement is still coarse for button-level, field-level, and data-scope rules. Introduce a centralized policy engine and permission metadata per action.
Tenancy `tenant.js` already resolves business, store, warehouse, and host context; ERP queries already scope by business. Store-level and warehouse-level row filtering is not yet consistently enforced across all domains. Multi-business support should evolve from implicit scoping into explicit tenant policy middleware.
Data model ERP tables already cover brands, customers, suppliers, inventory, stock movements, sales, sale items, POS holds, expenses, and attendance. No full purchase order lifecycle, invoice revisions, accounting ledger, returns engine, transfer approvals, or immutable audit partitioning yet. The database should evolve domain by domain instead of being rebuilt from scratch.
Backend stack Express 5 and `pg` are already live and power current admin behavior. Your requested target stack is NestJS + Prisma, which is not yet present in the repository. Use a strangler migration: preserve routes and tokens while moving ERP domains into NestJS modules.

Non-negotiables

Hard constraints

Application boundary

The ERP must remain inside the same SPA and the same admin route tree. That means routes such as `/admin/dashboard`, `/admin/pos`, `/admin/inventory`, `/admin/reports`, and `/admin/settings` remain the public contract for navigation.

No iframe, no parallel frontend, no second admin portal, and no subdomain handoff should exist anywhere in the user journey.

Identity and session

The current login remains the only entry point. After the user signs in once, the session should authorize every ERP module through a shared token, shared permission payload, and shared tenant scope.

Users should never need a second credential prompt just because they moved from storefront administration into retail operations.

Operational safety

Stock can never be overwritten in place. Every stock change must create a movement record with actor, source document, warehouse, quantity delta, and resulting balance.

The same rule applies to invoice edits, returns, voids, cancellations, and payment corrections: use reversal and adjustment records, not destructive edits.

Target application architecture

Same website, deeper platform

Integrated route and auth flow

Target service and event architecture under the same domain

Frontend structure

The current `AdminDashboard.jsx` should become the permanent enterprise shell. Split it into a stable layout layer, a route registry, a permission-aware navigation registry, and domain-specific lazy-loaded module packages such as `pos`, `inventory`, `purchases`, `returns`, `reports`, `settings`, and `super-admin`.

Each module should expose its own route children, data hooks, action permissions, keyboard shortcuts, and page-level skeleton states. The shell owns layout, breadcrumb, tenant switcher, notification center, and global quick search. The domains own business workflows.

ERP domain modules

Bounded contexts
Domain Owns Key entities Special rules
POS Billing Cart, billing, payments, holds, drafts, invoices, returns at counter Sale, sale item, payment allocation, bill hold, invoice sequence, terminal Atomic bill posting, split payment support, offline-safe draft recovery, printer profiles by terminal
Inventory Catalog stock, variants, batches, serials, movement ledger, reorder logic Inventory item, variant, batch, serial, movement, stock snapshot No direct stock overwrite; every change becomes a movement entry
Warehouse Warehouse master, transfer, count, adjustment, rack and shelf layout Warehouse, transfer, count session, adjustment, rack, shelf Approval workflow for transfers and irreversible count closure
Customers and CRM Customer identity, ledger, loyalty, credit, membership, notes Customer, loyalty account, credit account, communication note Customer financial balances must reconcile to finance and sales
Suppliers and Purchases Supplier master, purchase order, GRN, purchase return, landed cost Supplier, PO, GRN, bill, purchase return, allocation line Receiving creates stock movements and payable entries together
Accounting and Expenses Expense booking, payment reconciliation, store cash, ledgers, tax basis Expense, ledger entry, payment, cash session, tax summary ERP actions emit accounting events instead of manual double entry duplication
Reports Operational reports, dashboards, exports, scheduled summaries Snapshot, report definition, export job, report filter preset Large exports run in BullMQ and stream status back over WebSockets
Settings and Super Admin Business setup, stores, warehouses, taxes, printers, roles, audit, platform policies Business, store, tax profile, printer profile, role policy, audit record Super Admin can cross tenant boundaries; business roles cannot

RBAC and tenant isolation

Every action protected
Role gate
100%
Page gate
100%
Action gate
100%
API gate
100%

Role model

The baseline roles you listed are correct: Super Admin, Business Owner, Store Admin, Store Manager, Cashier, Warehouse Manager, Accountant, and Employee. Keep the role names stable for business meaning, but back them with permission bundles and data scopes instead of hardcoding behavior inside components.

Permission granularity

Each module should declare route permissions, screen permissions, widget visibility permissions, and action permissions such as `sale.void`, `inventory.adjust`, `purchase.approve`, `report.export`, and `settings.update_tax`. Buttons should disappear when forbidden, but the API must still reject the action server-side.

Data scope

Role permission is not enough. Every query also needs a data scope of `platform`, `business`, `store`, `warehouse`, or `self`. This avoids the common failure mode where a cashier is allowed to open a page but can still query another store’s data via filters or direct API access.

Required policy model

Platform scope
Super Admin
Business scope
Owner
Store scope
Store roles
Warehouse scope
Warehouse roles
Self scope
Employee

Data platform and migration

From current backend to target backend
Current runtime
Express
Already powering admin endpoints
Target runtime
NestJS
Introduced without changing the user-facing admin shell

Migration strategy

Do not stop the world and rewrite everything. Keep the current Express backend alive for existing commerce endpoints while introducing a NestJS app for ERP domains behind the same Nginx host. Nginx continues to serve the existing frontend and same-domain API paths, but progressively routes selected ERP endpoints to NestJS modules.

The cleanest progression is: first standardize a shared JWT contract, then move ERP domains one at a time into NestJS with Prisma, then retire the matching Express handlers once parity is verified. This gives you the target stack without forcing a separate ERP product or a dangerous all-at-once migration.

Recommended persistence rules

Concern Recommendation Reason
Primary database PostgreSQL on Neon with Prisma schema versioning Strong transactional integrity for sales, stock, approvals, and audit data
ERP IDs Use stable internal UUIDs plus human-facing business sequences for bills, invoices, PO, GRN, and transfers Business documents need readable numbers, but joins must rely on immutable keys
Stock ledger Separate current balance from movement history and count snapshots Fast reads plus perfect traceability
Audit logs Append-only table with actor, role, business, store, warehouse, IP, device, action, before-state, after-state, and reference document Supports investigations, approvals, and financial traceability
File assets Cloudinary for media and generated document storage, referenced by ERP entities Centralizes product images, invoices, attachments, and import files

Realtime, jobs, and integrations

Operational backbone

Realtime

Use WebSockets for live billing counters, cashier activity, stock alerts, queue status, and export job progress. The frontend shell should subscribe once after login and receive tenant-scoped channels based on business, store, and terminal.

Background jobs

BullMQ should handle report generation, PDF invoice creation, barcode batch generation, import validation, notification fanout, backup verification, and scheduled reconciliation. Any heavy export or multi-store report should never block the request-response thread.

Device and printer layer

Printer and scanner support belongs in a terminal profile layer. The browser captures keyboard-scanner and camera-scanner flows, while printer settings map bill format, label format, and terminal capabilities per store or cashier station.

Security and audit

Enterprise controls
Control What to implement Where it applies
Session model JWT access token + refresh token rotation + device registration + forced logout All admin and ERP sessions
Step-up protection 2FA or OTP for role changes, void bills, refund approval, and business setting changes High-risk ERP actions
Input hardening Validation pipes, DTO schemas, SQL parameterization, upload validation, output encoding Every API and import/export flow
CSRF and rate limiting Same-site cookie protections where needed, API throttling, IP anomaly detection, and lockout thresholds Auth, exports, password flows, and notification endpoints
Immutable audit Append-only records for create, edit, delete, print, download, login, logout, stock, settings, returns, and cancellations All ERP domains

Delivery path

Low-risk rollout
Wave Primary outcome Modules Exit condition
Wave 1 Stabilize foundation Admin shell refactor, route registry, policy engine, tenant context, session hardening, audit pipeline Every route and action is policy-checked and tenant-safe
Wave 2 Launch retail operations core POS Billing, Inventory, Products, Categories, Brands, Customers, basic Returns, barcode and invoice engine A store can bill, print, return, and reconcile stock from the same admin shell
Wave 3 Expand enterprise workflows Suppliers, Purchases, GRN, Warehouse transfers, Expenses, Employees, Attendance, Notifications Operational back-office runs without spreadsheets for daily work
Wave 4 Finish platform controls Advanced Reports, Business Settings, Store Management, Role Management, Super Admin, backup and restore, multi-business governance Platform scales to many businesses and stores with controlled isolation

First build slice

Best next move

Recommended immediate implementation slice

Start with Wave 1 plus the first half of Wave 2: stabilize the admin shell, formalize permission and tenant policy, then build a production-grade POS + Inventory core. That slice creates the highest business value earliest because it turns the current ERP shell into something operationally real without forcing accounting and warehouse complexity on day one.

  1. Refactor the admin shell into modular route registration with lazy-loaded domain packages.
  2. Introduce a centralized policy layer for page, action, and data-scope enforcement.
  3. Build the POS transaction engine with holds, split payments, returns, invoice printing, and cashier session tracking.
  4. Build the stock ledger with movements, adjustments, reorder rules, batches, and warehouse-aware balances.
  5. Then migrate those ERP APIs into NestJS + Prisma behind the same domain, preserving the existing login and route surface.
Generated for the selected workspace Integrated ERP Admin Blueprint · 2026-07-12
